html:
Cotton Gin Roofs and Rail-Town Buildings in Gilliam

Gilliam sits up in north Caddo Parish along the old Kansas City Southern rail line, in cotton country that's been ginning crops for generations. The buildings we service here are working buildings first — a cotton gin, seed storage, farm equipment sheds — and the roofs over them have to hold up against lint, dust, and machinery vibration in a way a standard commercial roof never has to.

A Gin Roof Isn't a Warehouse Roof

Cotton gin buildings are typically long-span metal structures with high clearance for the ginning equipment, and the roof takes a beating from more than weather. Cotton lint is highly combustible, and it accumulates on any horizontal surface it can reach, including roof purlins, curbs, and the underside of panels near equipment penetrations. That means the roofing details around dust collector vents, cyclone separators, and exhaust penetrations matter more here than they would on almost any other building type we service.

We also see more vibration-related fastener and seam issues on gin roofs than on nearly any other building, because the ginning equipment runs hard during harvest season and the whole structure shakes with it for weeks at a time. A roof that was tight in the off-season can develop loosened fasteners and open seams by the time the next crop comes through.

Seasonal Loads Change the Maintenance Calendar

Unlike most commercial roofs, a gin roof's hardest use is compressed into a few months a year during harvest, and the quiet season is the right time to catch up on maintenance before the next run. We recommend gin owners have the roof checked in the off-season specifically — after the equipment has stopped running and before the next crop comes in — rather than waiting for a leak to show up mid-harvest when the building can least afford downtime.

A leak during ginning season is more than a maintenance inconvenience. Water intrusion near electrical equipment or into stored seed can shut down a line during the only weeks of the year the building earns money, so we treat pre-season inspection requests with the urgency they deserve.

What We Check Before and After Ginning Season

  • Fastener backout and panel seam condition after a season of equipment vibration
  • Lint and dust accumulation around penetrations, curbs, and dust collector connections
  • Flashing and boot condition at cyclone separator and exhaust stack penetrations
  • Corrosion from ginning chemicals or moisture at low points and gutters
  • Overall panel and structural condition ahead of the next harvest cycle

Rail-Line and Farm-Supply Buildings Nearby

Gilliam's other commercial buildings sit along or near the old rail corridor — farm supply, small storage buildings, a scattering of older commercial structures that were built when the rail line still moved more freight through town. Those roofs are typically simpler metal roofs without the dust and vibration concerns of a working gin, but they're old enough now that fastener and flashing maintenance matters just as much, if not more, given their age.

A number of these older buildings were put up before anyone was thinking about R-value or continuous insulation, and adding coverage during a recover is often the single biggest improvement we can make on a building like that — not because code requires it on every job, but because a bare metal roof with no thermal break runs hot in summer and sweats on the underside in winter, and both of those shorten the life of whatever's stored under it.

North Caddo Weather and Cotton Country

This part of Caddo Parish gets the same severe spring weather as the rest of the region, and a tall gin building with a long open roof span catches wind differently than a low warehouse does. Hail is a real concern for panel damage, and straight-line wind can find weak seams on a large roof faster than it would on a smaller structure. We factor building height and span into how we assess wind damage risk out here, rather than square footage alone.

Wind uplift on a gin roof also behaves differently at the eave than it does on a boxier building, because the long clear-span structure and the high sidewalls common to ginning equipment create pressure differences a standard commercial building doesn't have. We look at eave and corner fastening patterns specifically on these roofs rather than assuming a field-inspection of the mid-roof panels tells the whole story.

Gilliam Roofing Questions We Hear From Gin Operators

When's the best time to have the gin roof inspected?

Right after ginning season ends and before the next planting cycle, while the equipment is quiet and the roof can be checked without interfering with operations.

Can you work around an active ginning schedule if something needs attention mid-season?

We can, and we prioritize gin calls during harvest specifically because downtime during those weeks costs more than it would the rest of the year. Tell us what's happening and we'll get out as fast as the situation calls for.

Does cotton dust actually damage roofing materials?

It doesn't damage the panel itself much, but it holds moisture against seals, flashing, and fasteners, which speeds up deterioration in those spots. Clearing dust from penetrations is a real maintenance step, well beyond ordinary housekeeping.

How do you handle a roof this tall and this long-span?

The same fundamentals apply — fastener, seam, and flashing condition — but we account for the added wind exposure and access requirements a taller, longer building brings.

Do you do smaller farm buildings too, or only the gin?

Yes, farm equipment sheds and storage buildings are a regular part of what we service in Gilliam alongside the gin itself.
